utterances: Who turns out to be his daughter.
Yeah.
Let's get Ken on the line, Ken calling us from Shelburne Falls.
Hello.
Hi there.
Hi, I just have an anecdote about the one time I was moved to call a How's My Driving number, which was some time ago.
I was following a 18-wheeler truck home, and for miles and miles and miles of bad winter driving, it wasn't letting about 20 cars go past it, and it was going very, very slow.
And after a long, long time, I was right up behind it and I saw the number.
And I thought, well, I'm going to call this number; this is the time.
And so I jotted the number down, and I got home, and I thought, well, at least, you know, I'm home safe, and I can call this number, and there's some justice in the world.
And I called the number, and it was a phone sex line.
(Soundbite of laughter)
No, no, but -

I had a similar experience in my own driving here in Chicago where I had an experience with an extremely aggressive driver of a big rig and saw that he did have a placard and that he had crossed out the last four numbers so that you could only dial the first - the 1-800-555, and then everything else had been blacked out.

And I thought, well, that suggests that this How's My Driving system is working because this is an aggressive driver who knows he's an aggressive driver and has taken the trouble of trying to thwart the system.
Now obviously one hopes that his employers will ultimately realize what he's up to.
Hmm, let's get Mark on the line, Mark calling us from New Jersey.
Hi, how you doing?
OK.
I don't think it's snitching at all.
I think it's establishing social accountability.
Just like checking for child abuse in hospitals if you bring your kid in.
I've experienced it and, you know, it's a broken wrist.
Yeah, sure, he fell down the steps.
I don't mind them grilling me about that.
But my experience with the traffic sticker on the truck has been very good from both directions.
I think it breaks the myth of public anonymity and because dangerous driving is everybody's business.
That seems to be a large part of your point, Lior Strahilevitz.
It is.
I mean the traffic accidents are the leading cause of death for Americans aged 15 to 29.
Globally, 1.2 million people die every year from traffic accidents.

If we can get anything even closely approximating the 20 to 53 percent reductions in accidents that the commercial fleets seem to be experiencing, according to the best studies we've got, then we could save thousands, tens of thousands of lives.
I don't know if there's any collected data on it, but I think it's repetitive bad behavior that really causes a lot of accidents.
And if you can get this behavior to stop before it runs into another car, I think it's better.
Thanks very much, Mark.
I do...
Thank you.
Yeah.
I was just going to add I think that's right.
The data that I've looked at from the traffic scholars and from the insurance industry suggests that really 80 to 90 percent of American drivers are basically safe, courteous, and skilled.
But there are these bad apples and they're consistent, right.
They're bad apples over time.
Now, the status quo says well, we'll identify those once they crash into someone.
How's My Driving - the advantage of it - the reason why it seems to work well with fleets is that you can identify them earlier, more accurately.

And as Jill suggested you can deter some of the worst driving, which probably some people listening to TALK OF THE NATION right now are seeing in front of them, right - someone swerving from one lane to another unnecessarily, someone incessantly hitting their brakes, someone tailgating right behind them.
It's frustrating.
It's angering.
And currently we have no system for collecting the feedback that many motorists, I think, would like to provide.
Lior Strahilevitz thanks very much for being with us today.
Always a pleasure.
Thank you.
Lior Strahilevitz is an assistant professor of law at the University of Chicago Law School and he joined us today from a studio on the campus at the University of Chicago.
Alexandra Natapoff has written extensively about snitching, particularly relating to government and the criminal justice system.
She's an associate professor of law at Loyola Law School in Los Angeles.
She's now with us on the phone from her home in L.A.
Nice to have you on the program today.
Thanks so much for having me.
Have we become a very mistrustful nation where everyone needs to watch everyone else?
Well, it's such an important topic to be discussing in this context and I'm so glad that you've asked me to weigh in.
I think that it's important to look at the nature of the information and the interaction in any given informant or snitching program.
You're caller, Mark, who spoke a moment ago who said that this, you know, the How's My Driving, that's not snitching, that's social accountability.

Well, sometimes getting more information into the right hands can be a positive thing, but sometimes - as you've mentioned I've written about snitching in the criminal justice system - sometimes institutionalized snitching can be a very negative thing.

And so I think it's - we shouldn't be too hasty to write the whole informing or surveillance question off or to embrace it fully, but to look carefully at the kind of relationship that snitching and informing and surveillance threatens and decide whether it's worth it at any given time to institute that kind of program.
Well, if those statistics are right, for example that How's My Driving bumper stickers reduces accidents by significant percentages that seems to be an unalloyed good.
It does seem very good, but that's only one piece of the puzzle.

One of the reasons that How's My Driving may be a very good program is not merely that it deters bad driving, because in our criminal justice system, and more generally in our system of laws, preventing the bad thing from happening is only half of the equation.
The other half of the equation is what do we give up by having such a program.
And from Professor Strahilevitz's description and just our comments and understanding of driving, it sounds like we don't give up a whole lot by having such a program.
I don't need to trust the person driving in the next lane on the freeway.
I only need him to obey the rules of the road.
Well, right now, certainly in the near term, they're going to come from the active Army close combat units.
Now essentially we're talking about ground forces, infantry soldiers, who are parts of what are called brigade combat teams.
Whereas, in other scenarios we may be giving up a lot more by instituting informant programs, snitching programs and so forth, because it does call to question the trust into play.
You asked, for example, Ms. Starishevsky in the program, how do you think the nannies feel walking around with license plates on their toddler carriers?
Right.
And I thought, implicitly, what you were asking her is there any downside to asking complete strangers to surveil the person that you've entrusted you child to.
And I think the snitching question, the informant, and the surveillance question raises central issues about how we police ourselves and what are the appropriate methods that we should use to do so.
We're talking about the snitch society.
You're listening to TALK OF THE NATION from NPR News.
And let's see if we can get another caller on the line.
This is Dez(ph).
Dez calling us from South Dakota.
Yes.
Thank you for taking my call.
Sure.
I'm old enough to remember the Soviet Union and growing up and hearing these stories about the KGB and what made the KGB so bad was that they encouraged turning in your neighbor.
Or your mom or your child.
Yes, exactly.
Now, someone who believes it is OK today, you know, because we are defined by our actions, then was the KGB just in what they did?
Well, we're a long way from How's My Nanny to the KGB, but I wonder, Alexandra Natapoff, is this the top of a slippery slope?
Neal, I think it is.
And again that's why it's so important to be specific about what use of snitches or informants are we talking about.

As Dez, your caller, mentioned we have very disturbing evidence that the pervasive use of informants to maintain the rules and to enforce the law can be extremely destructive, as Dez mentioned the Soviet Union and the KGB.
I've written a little bit about the East German police, the Stasi, who are infamous for using informants.
And we now have psychological profiles of the individuals who lived through that era.
